questions.description: Critically analyse the tort of passing off and discuss whether such an action is relevant in the modern commercial…
questions.text: Critically analyse the tort of passing off and discuss whether such an action is relevant in the modern commercial world.

questions.description: Lee was on holiday, enjoying the pleasures of Apollo Lake, a small lake in… Advise Lee and Deirdre as to any tort claim…
questions.text: Lee was on holiday, enjoying the pleasures of Apollo Lake, a small lake in the midlands, often used by tourists for many water sports activities, such as waterskiing and so forth. Lee himself used a jet ski. Late one evening, he decided to have a moonlight ride on the jet-ski with his girlfriend, Deirdre. As they were enjoying themselves, the jet-ski suddenly stopped. No matter what Lee did, he could not restart the jet-ski. Realising that there was no one else on the lake and that they could not stay there for much longer before feeling the impact of hypothermia, Lee and Deirdre began to swim to the nearest shoreline. Once they were on dry land, Lee quickly realised that he had no idea where they were, but they were definitely far away from where they parked their car. Accordingly, Lee felt that they should head inland and try to find any house where they parked their car. Accordingly, Lee felt that they should head inland and try to find any house where they could make a phone call for help. Lee and Deirdre crossed a number of hedgerows and fences without too much difficulty before encountering a long, high wall which blocked their path. As there was no way around the wall, Lee and Deirdre climbed over it with great difficulty, Lee cutting his arm in the process from where some shards of broken glass had been embedded on the top of the wall. Bleeding profusely, both Lee and Deirdre began running as fast as possible when they could hear the sound of some dogs barking coming towards them at speed. In the pitch black, and seized with fear from the dogs which appeared to be drawing very close, Lee was grateful for the relatively smooth terrain compared to the other fields that they had encountered. Suddenly Deirdre screamed as she fell into what appeared to be a hole in the ground and broke her leg. Meanwhile the dogs had caught up with Lee and one had bitten him before his handler called him off. Lee discovered that they had wandered onto the local golf course and that Deirdre had fallen into a bunker. Advise Lee and Deirdre as to any tort claim they might have against the owners of the golf course.

questions.description: John owned a large motorcycle that he enjoyed riding on the remote roads… Advise John as to whether he is the cause of…
questions.text: John owned a large motorcycle that he enjoyed riding on the remote roads in Connemara. He meticulously maintained the motorcycle as it was his pride and joy. One day, John decided that he would do a tour of the country, staying in campsites overnight and moving on the next day, living the nomadic life he had been attracted to when watching television shows. He loaded up the motorcycle with a small tent and enough clothing and other supplies to keep him for about three weeks. It was a struggle to get everything packed onto the motorcycle, but he managed. When his friend Jane heard what he was doing, she insisted that she come along as well. John did not really want her to do so, but Jane was insistent and John relented saying to her: "OK, but you will have to look after yourself. And you cannot take much with you, the bike is already well loaded with my stuff." Jane agreed but found that there were so many things that she needed, it was difficult to leave anything behind. John and Jane were getting on exceptionally well, and John decided that he would show off to Jane and began to overtake a vehicle on a back road doing over 120km/h. As John passed the vehicle, the bike began to wobble and John lost control, resulting in Jane being thrown from the motorcycle resulting in severe injury. The evidence shows that John was speeding and that the bike was overloaded. However, the evidence also showed that unknown to anyone, the rear tyre had a slow puncture from an embedded nail and that this would ultimately have produced a similar wobble in the ordinary use of the motorcycle resulting in a loss of control of the motorcycle. Advise John as to whether he is the cause of any harm to Jane under the law of tort.

questions.description: With increasing automation, machines are now programmed to make decisions…
questions.text: With increasing automation, machines are now programmed to make decisions that previously would have been left in the hands of a human operator. Critically discuss the extent that there is statutory protection for defective products, paying particular attention to liability for design defect in such products.

questions.description: Robert had been feeling unwell for a number of days and made an… Negligenc.
questions.text: Robert had been feeling unwell for a number of days and made an appointment with his local doctor. On examination, his doctor said that he was concerned about Robert's symptoms as they could represent some deeper underlying issue. Accordingly he recommended that Robert be referred to a national hospital for further tests. At the hospital, Robert underwent a battery of tests although no doctor or nurse would tell him what they thought might be wrong. The only comment was from a trainee nurse who said to him while escorting him to the X-Ray department, that he must be seriously sick given all the testing that was being done. She quickly withdrew the comment as Robert became agitated and he calmed down when told by a doctor that it was the student nurses first day on the job -- and likely to be her last if she did that again. About two weeks later, Robert received a letter in the post from the hospital, telling him that he had been diagnosed with a terminal disease and asking him to come in to meet with their counsellors who could guide him through his final days. Shocked and distraught, he met with the counsellors who advised him to prepare a will and make funeral arrangements. As he left the hospital, he was so depressed that he wanted to end it all and threw himself at an oncoming vehicle, causing him to break his leg. On arrival back at accident and emergency, one of the doctors who had been testing Robert said, "Back again so soon, and we just having given you a clean bill of health. Oh well." Robert was shocked when it transpired that due to negligence, the hospital had sent out an incorrect set of results. Advise Robert whether he has any action in tort for the negligent notification of his test results.

questions.description: Janice worked for a car sales dealership, which specialised in selling…
questions.text: Janice worked for a car sales dealership, which specialised in selling unusual and hard to come by vehicles. Chandler, the owner of the dealership was not an active manager, leaving a lot of the decisions to his employees, other than that of price, which he strictly controlled. Beyond that however, he pretty much allowed his employees to run the car dealership as they knew best. Chandler had just left for a holiday on an Arctic expedition and had left strict instructions not to be disturbed for any reason. Joey came into the dealership looking to buy an expensive sports car. When Janice had agreed a price with Joey for the car, Joey asked would she take a 20 foot motorboat as part exchange. Janice was unsure, as they had never taken anything like this before, although they had taken things like motor homes and, she even remembered once having taken a mobile home in part-exchange. Still, she felt this was sufficiently unusual that she would have to get approval from Chandler. However, Chandler was not due back for four weeks. Joey was disappointed but he understood. However, he asked Janice if he could leave the boat parked on her sales lot while waiting for a decision, rather than driving it to his home. Janice hesitated but keen to give every chance to making this sale, she agreed. Joey left the boat at the front of the dealership. Some days later, Janice heard that there was violent storm headed towards the dealership, so she decided to tow the boat back to Joey's house. Unfortunately, just as she had pushed the boat onto the side of the main road in preparation for hitching it to her truck, a gust of wind from the storm blew the boat onto a neighbouring property, causing significant damage. Advise Chandler if he is liable for the actions of Janice in any tort action that might arise as a result of the harm to the neighbours' property.

questions.description: Critically discuss the liability in tort for the negligent discharge of…
questions.text: Critically discuss the liability in tort for the negligent discharge of both mandatory statutory obligations and discretionary powers by public authorities.

questions.description: Billy owns a public house in a small village in the countryside. Duty Of Care.
questions.text: Billy owns a public house in a small village in the countryside. For a variety of reasons his clientele has been steadily shrinking, So much so that he knows virtually all his regular clients by name. In an attempt to increase business in the pub, Billy has run a Valentine's Night event. Essentially, whenever someone buys a drink, they will get another drink of the same value free if they can show they are celebrating Valentine's night with a loved one. Word spreads through the town like wildfire and on Valentine's night Billy's pub is packed. Billy is taken aback. He had thought that the offer would be popular but had not anticipated the sheer numbers of people involved. Overwhelmed, Billy does not bother checking whether any individual ordering a drink has a date but simply puts up two drinks for every order received. He also does the same for Larry, a regular who is at the pub every night. By the end of the evening, Billy is exhausted and Larry is completely inebriated. As Billy begins clearing the pub, Larry asks if he can stay the night as he does not feel that he is in a fit condition to drive home. Billy is taken aback, and says "Go on a way out of that, you're grand. You cannot stay here, that's for sure." As Larry gets up, he stumbles to the floor and again asks Billy if he can stay. Billy says "Sure listen, it's only a couple of miles, you'll be grand." Larry asks Billy to keep his car keys until the morning as he says that he doesn't trust himself to drive, but Billy does not want the responsibility and says "If you are able to know that you shouldn't drive, you are not likely to drive, now are you." Larry replies with "I suppose that's true!" Larry loses control on the drive home and is severely injured. Advise Billy if he owes Larry a duty of care under the law of tort.

questions.description: Alice was employed by Nanocyte Technology Ltd as a clerical assistant. Company.
questions.text: Alice was employed by Nanocyte Technology Ltd as a clerical assistant. Due to a downturn in the economy, Alice was made redundant. Alice was particularly upset at this as she felt that she was singled out by Frank, her line manager, whom she believed never liked her. Her former colleagues in the company organised a private party to mark her departure from the company. There were about twenty or so people there. Alice had a little too much to drink and began to perform a skit where she mimicked Frank, copying his accent, wearing glasses just like his and so on. She also portrayed him as a lecherous adulterer and as an embezzler. At the end, Alice passed out and had no recollection of what transpired that evening. Alice's friend Jennifer had filmed the event on her smartphone and had immediately posted it to her social network site under the label "Alice does Frank". Frank's wife Ellen saw the video on the site and was horrified, believing Frank must have had an affair with Alice. Alice is also distraught, as she never intended the skit to be shown publicly and besides she never expressly referred to Frank during the video clip. Jennifer immediately removed the video clip from the site when Alice asked her to do so. Advise Frank if he has any action in tort with respect to his reputation against either Alice or Jennifer.

questions.description: The successful litigation of a tort claim in negligence requires that the… Negligenc.
questions.text: The successful litigation of a tort claim in negligence requires that the plaintiff establish that the defendant has failed to reach the appropriate standard of care. Critically discuss the approach of the Irish courts in establishing the requisite standard of care.

questions.description: Sally was walking down Main Street when she saw her boyfriend Alex across… Negligenc, Damages.
questions.text: Sally was walking down Main Street when she saw her boyfriend Alex across the street. She waved to him and crossed over to meet him. When they finished talking, she kissed Alex goodbye, and walked on. Then she heard Alex call to her and as she turned around, was horrified when she saw a number of concrete blocks fall on top of him. Sally ran to him and began to try to pull Alex from the rubble. As she pulled off the first couple of blocks she could only hear his muffled screams getting fainter. Suddenly she lost her footing on the rubble and broke her ankle. She was certain that Alex had been killed and that she would be next. Fortunately they were both rescued. However, it was a long and expensive recovery in terms of medical bills and Sally suffered depression, reliving the events as horrific nightmares, night after night. Moreover, since she was unable to work, she could not afford to continue renting her apartment and had to move back with her parents. As a result she lost her deposit for breaching the lease. The defendant, a building company that had been working on a second floor apartment, admitted liability and conceded that they had been in gross violation of several building regulations. In fact they have a track record of such violations and this is not the first time they have been involved in such negligence. Advise Sally on the different types of damages that she may be able to claim in any tort actions arising from these events.

questions.description: Whether the limitation period for a tort action should run from when the…
questions.text: Whether the limitation period for a tort action should run from when the harm occurs or from when the plaintiff becomes aware of the harm represents an important policy choice balancing a number of competing interests. Critically discuss the manner in which the Irish legislature resolved this issue.

questions.description: Harry was recently made redundant. Negligenc.
questions.text: Harry was recently made redundant. He decided to set up his own business selling firewood. Harry offered to cut trees for neighbouring farmers for free provided that he could take the cut trees for himself. He would then take these trees and cut them into firewood. He stored the wood in some sheds that were in his back garden. He would then put them into bags and sell them directly to the public. Business had not gone well the first year, with many of his customers complaining that the firewood did not burn well. After talking to others in the business, they recommended heating the sheds to accelerate drying out the firewood. As a result, Harry installed a low power electrical heating system to aid in drying out the firewood. During the summer, there was a thunder and lightning storm. The lightning struck the electrical generator causing a power surge in the circuit. As the fuse blew, a spark from the wiring set fire to some of the firewood in Harry's sheds. When Harry heard the smoke alarm he immediately set about trying to put it out. Unfortunately, some of the hot ash from the fire drifted into his neighbour's property where it landed on a 5 litre petrol can next to their car. The petrol can exploded, damaging the vehicle beyond repair. Advise Harry if, in the absence of any negligence on his part, he has any liability in tort arising from these events.

questions.description: Intentional torts such as trespass and false imprisonment are anachronistic throwbacks to the past and are no longer…
questions.text: Intentional torts such as trespass and false imprisonment are anachronistic throwbacks to the past and are no longer required in modern tort law. Critically discuss this statement, paying particular attention to the specific elements necessary for an intentional tort.

questions.description: Global Remedy manufactures various medical devices, particularly in the… Company.
questions.text: Global Remedy manufactures various medical devices, particularly in the field of nicotine addiction. They have been in business for the last twenty years and have proven to be very successful, with an extensive domestic and export market. One of their most successful products is a small bio-degradable plastic device which is implanted under the skin and which emits a slow release nicotine substitute, allowing the patient to break their addiction to smoking. Global Remedy does not actually manufacture this product but in fact assembles the component parts from two suppliers: the pharmaceutical company which supplies the actual medication and the manufacturer which makes the bio-degradable plastic container. Global Remedy has recently been informed by the supplier of the bio-degradable plastic element that there may be an issue with the quality of the plastic that could result in it disintegrating. If that were the case, the effect would be to release all the medication in one go which might cause complications for the patient. Unfortunately there is no way of determining if the plastic element of any particular container is defective without removing it for inspection. Jill had been trying to quit smoking for years and finally had one of these devices implanted into her arm. One day while surfing on the internet, she came across various sites which alleged that Global Remedy was trying to cover up that these devices were dangerous. Jill now wants the device removed immediately, although there is no evidence that it has malfunctioned. Advise Jill as whether she has any action in tort against the manufacturer of the device.

questions.description: Daniel is a well-known financial advisor. Advise Mary if she has any action in tort against Daniel as a result of his…
questions.text: Daniel is a well-known financial advisor. He has his own radio show where he dispenses advice on investing in stock markets around the world. He also writes numerous articles for newspapers and magazines which are very popular and he has a huge following. In addition, Daniel operates a private consulting firm where for a set fee he offers specific advice on what companies to invest in. Many of his peers have however expressed concerns about his unorthodox views where he often takes a contrarian view to the conventional wisdom which is not based on any real analysis of his own. It is widely believed that Daniel operates on his 'gut' feeling although his success rate compares well with his more traditional competitors. Recently Daniel has taken to Twitter and will tweet various comments designed to attract people to both his radio show and consulting firm. Two weeks ago, Daniel misread a newspaper article which suggested that the European Central Bank would begin raising interest rates rapidly. He figured that if interest rates went up, this would be good for bank profits. Daniel tweeted the following: "Interest rates going up, banks to profit, buy, buy, buy Provincial Bank shares NOW #makeamillionwithdaniel" Mary had been following Daniel since he began tweeting and had just come into an inheritance from her deceased mother. She invested it all in Provincial Bank shares as a result of Daniel's tweet. Unfortunately, the ECB had in fact said that they would not raise interest rates rapidly and the share price of all banks fell in value. Provincial Bank suffered more than most and Mary lost about 40% of the value of her investment. Advise Mary if she has any action in tort against Daniel as a result of his actions.
